title: Did PEPFAR crowd out non-HIV clinical research? Quasi-experimental evidence from 15 African countries
body: Did PEPFAR displace non-HIV clinical research in recipient African countries? We applied three quasi-experimental methods to ClinicalTrials.gov data from 2000 to 2025 across ten PEPFAR-focus countries and five non-PEPFAR comparators. Interrupted time series estimated the level change and slope change in non-HIV trial starts at the 2004 PEPFAR implementation point. Difference-in-differences compared annual trial growth between PEPFAR and control countries with sensitivity analyses across four post-period windows. A synthetic control constructed a counterfactual Uganda from weighted non-PEPFAR donors. The ITS showed a measurable shift in non-HIV trial trajectory at PEPFAR onset, while DiD estimates varied by post-period window and trial category. The synthetic control gap for Uganda provided complementary country-level evidence. These findings extend the Bendavid and Bhatt 2009 health service crowding-out analysis to the research domain but carry ecological and registration-bias limitations that preclude definitive causal claims.

sentences.text: Did PEPFAR funding measurably displace non-HIV clinical trial activity in recipient African countries relative to non-PEPFAR comparators?
We queried ClinicalTrials.gov for annual interventional trial starts from 2000 to 2025 across ten PEPFAR-focus countries and five non-PEPFAR African comparators.
Three quasi-experimental designs were applied: interrupted time series with segmented regression, difference-in-differences with sensitivity windows, and synthetic control for Uganda.
The ITS detected a shift in non-HIV trial trajectory at the PEPFAR onset, while DiD estimates varied by post-period window and trial classification.
Sensitivity analyses across four post-period windows and the synthetic control approach provided complementary evidence on the direction and magnitude of the effect.
These findings extend the Bendavid and Bhatt health service crowding-out evidence to the clinical research domain in Africa.
Ecological design, ClinicalTrials.gov registration bias, short pre-period, and unmeasured confounders including GDP growth and concurrent aid programs preclude definitive causal claims.

outsideNote.data: ClinicalTrials.gov API v2, 10 PEPFAR-focus + 5 non-PEPFAR countries, 2000-2025

ai_transparency: LLM assistance was used for code generation and early drafting. All quasi-experimental methods, parameter choices, and interpretations were specified and reviewed by the author, who takes full responsibility for the content.

references.desc: Bendavid & Bhatt (2009). Does PEPFAR crowd out other health aid?
Abadie, Diamond & Hainmueller (2010). Synthetic Control Methods.
Bernal, Cummins & Gasparrini (2017). ITS design for policy evaluation.
